text: Diagnostic laparoscopy has become routine in many small animal practices, providing several advantages such as markedly improved visualization, precise and substantial biopsy procurement, speedy patient recovery, and the ability to document lesions with digital photographs or video.
More recently, however, veterinarians have begun to apply this technology as an alternative to a number of common surgical procedures, reaping similar benefits to the delight of many pet owners.
This video will demonstrate some of the most common and practical laparoscopic assisted surgeries performed in dogs and cats.
This video was developed to demonstrate laparoscopic instrumentation, technique, biopsy procedures, and selected surgical procedures.
Dr. Eric Monet, Chief of Small Animal Surgery at Colorado State University, will be joining me in demonstrating some of the laparoscopic procedures we teach at the center.
The varus needle is used for initial insufflation of the patient with CO2.
It consists of a sharp outer needle with an inner, blunt, spring-loaded stylet.
For use in thoracoscopy, when pneumothorax is not desired, the valve can be removed.
Shown here are rotating forceps where the instrument tip can be oriented in any position by simply rotating the knob near the handle of the instrument.
The curved Metzenbaum scissors are used for incising tissues.
Note the probe has one centimeter markings for estimating organ or lesion size.
Full thickness biopsies of the small intestine can be obtained by exteriorizing a segment of bowel through the abdominal wall.
Placement of the two trocars used in this procedure is dependent upon the surgeon's preference.
In this case, a right lateral approach is used with the portal sites similar to those used for liver biopsy.
Once the trocars have been placed, a segment of the intestine is identified for biopsy.
A 5 mm Babcock forceps is used to grasp the anti-mesenteric border of the intestine, which is then pulled to the cannula to assure that it can be easily exteriorized.
The cannula incision must be enlarged to accommodate the loop of bowel.
Under visual control with the laparoscope, a number 11 blade is passed alongside the cannula and the incision is extended.
This incision may be further enlarged with Metzenbaum scissors if desired.
It may also be helpful to place a GELPI retractor in the abdominal wall for easier access to the intestine.
The pneumoperitoneum is lost, and the cannula, forceps, and intestine are pulled through the abdominal wall, thus exteriorizing a small loop of intestine.
The anti-mesenteric border of the intestine is biopsied as it would be with an open surgical procedure.
At the completion of the intestinal biopsy, the intestine is returned to the abdomen.

If additional biopsies are required, the cannula is replaced through the incision site, and the incision is closed around the cannula using a towel clamp or purse string suture, and the pneumoperitoneum is reestablished.
Additional intestinal biopsies are obtained in a similar manner.
Acute pancreatitis is a common indication for laparoscopic assisted placement of jejunostomy tubes.
However, this procedure may be adapted to the placement of feeding tubes into other portions of the digestive tract for other reasons.
In this case, we'll use a right lateral approach, placing the tube into the duodenum and passing it into the proximal jejunum.
The distal anti-mesenteric border of the duodenum is grasped with Babcock forceps and exteriorized using the same method described for lap assisted intestinal biopsies.
The duodenum is pulled to the cannula.
The cannula incision is enlarged with a blade or Metzenbaum scissors.
A GELPI retractor is placed for easier access to the intestine
and the cannula, forceps, and intestine are pulled through the abdominal wall, exteriorizing a loop of intestine.
Two four-aught monofilament absorbable stay sutures are placed in the intestine to prevent it from falling back into the abdominal cavity.
A purse string suture is placed on the anti-mesenteric border of the intestine.
A number 11 blade is used to puncture the intestine in the center of the purse string.
And a five to eight French feeding tube is introduced in the loop of bowel and fed in an aboral direction to the proximal jejunum to a depth of 14 centimeters.
The purse string suture is closed
and the intestine is returned to the abdominal cavity except for the segment containing the feeding tube.
The intestine is then pexied to the abdominal wall with four simple interrupted sutures.
The abdominal wall, subcutaneous tissue, and skin are closed in a routine fashion.
And the tube is affixed to the skin of the abdominal wall.
This technique can easily be modified for placement of gastrostomy feeding tubes in the greater curvature of the stomach.
A laparoscope can be used to perform a minimally invasive gastropexy in patients that are at high risk for gastric dilatation with volvulus.
The antrum of the stomach is exteriorized through the right abdominal wall and then pexied to the musculature of the wall.
The animal is positioned in dorsal recumbency and the telescope portal is placed in the midline just caudal to the umbilicus.
An incision is made through the skin and subcutaneous tissue to the linea alba.
An endotip cannula is placed in the incision and rotated clockwise, applying slight pressure to the abdominal wall until it enters the peritoneal cavity.
The telescope may be placed in the cannula to visually determine when the abdominal cavity is entered.
Seen here on the right is the falciform ligament.
The second portal, which will be used for grasping the antrum, is placed on the right side approximately two to three centimeters behind the last rib and two to three centimeters off the midline.
In this case, a standard cannula with sharp trocar is used and visualized with the laparoscope internally upon entry.
The telescope is directed to the right anterior abdomen and the pylorus is identified.
A Babcock forceps is used to grasp the antrum midway between the lesser and greater curvature.
The stomach is then pulled close to the cannula.
If there's too much tension to bring the antrum to the body wall, then the antrum should be re-grasped closer to the body of the stomach.
Partial deflation of the abdomen is also helpful to reduce the tension on the stomach wall.
The instrument cannula incision is enlarged using a number 11 scalpel blade.
Gelpy retractors may be used to help keep the incision open.
Metzenbaum scissors are used to increase the opening to approximately five centimeters in length, in a direction parallel to the last rib.
The grasping forceps and cannula are then pulled through the abdominal wall simultaneously, exteriorizing the antrum of the stomach.
Two stay sutures are placed in the stomach wall to prevent it from falling back into the abdominal cavity.
An incisional gastropexy is then performed in the routine manner.
A 3 to 5 centimeter long incision is made through the serosa and muscularis layers using a number 15 scalpel blade.
Metzenbaum scissors are used to create two seromuscular flaps.
Using three-aught monofilament absorbable suture in a continuous pattern, the seromuscular flaps are pexied to the transverse abdominal muscle.
Once this is completed, the external and internal oblique muscles are closed with a continuous suture pattern
and the subcutaneous tissue and skin are closed in a routine manner.
The pneumoperitoneum is re-established through the telescope cannula and the PEXI site is inspected internally.
The telescope and cannula are then removed and the incision is closed.
Laparoscopic assisted cystoscopy allows placement of a rigid endoscope into the urinary bladder that has been partially exteriorized through the abdominal wall.
Using this technique, it is possible to examine and biopsy the lumen of the bladder and to remove calculi or polyps.
A urinary catheter is placed and connected to a bag of sterile fluids under pressure.
The patient is positioned in dorsal recumbency.
The telescope cannula is located on the abdominal midline, just cranial to the umbilicus.
And the instrument cannula is located slightly cranial to the urinary bladder.
The telescope is introduced and directed caudally to visualize the urinary bladder.
The bladder is distended with saline.
Babcock forceps are used to grasp the bladder at the apex and pull it up to the cannula.
The cannula incision is then enlarged, and the apex of the bladder is drawn up to the incision.
Gelpy retractors may be used to help keep the incision open.
Two stay sutures are placed to prevent it from falling back into the abdominal cavity.
The serosal layer of the bladder is temporarily pexied to the linea of the abdominal wall using two simple continuous sutures.
The instrument trocar and cannula are used to puncture the bladder wall.
An IV line is attached to the stopcock of the cannula for outflow and may be connected to suction if desired.
The telescope is inserted into the bladder.
The bladder mucosa and proximal urethra are examined with the telescope.
Forceps or suction tubes can be placed into the bladder alongside the telescope to obtain biopsies or remove calculi.
At the conclusion of the procedure, the bladder is closed in a standard manner and returned to the abdomen.
The cannula ports are then closed.
